fullArticle:
Masashi Hashimoto, a senior economist at the Institute for International Monetary Affairs (Provided by Masashi Hashimoto)

The yen plunged to a nearly 40-year low on June 30, hitting the 162-yen mark against the dollar in a historic slide that an expert said reflects decades of declining economic competitiveness and short-term policy pressures.

The stunning depreciation, which marks the currency’s weakest point since December 1986, is due to overlapping factors that have effectively created a “cheap Japan,” according to Masashi Hashimoto, a senior economist at the Institute for International Monetary Affairs, a Tokyo-based think tank.

“Thirty-nine years ago was an era when the Japanese economy was strong, and globally, the yen’s real strength was on an upward trajectory. Now, it is in a ...
